\title[Reading a Remote Teammate's Body]%
{Reading a Remote Teammate's Body: A Body-Worn Sensing Interface for Group Coordination in a Distributed Interactive Space}

\author{Anonymous Author(s)}

%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Networked multiplayer games are among the most inhabited yet distributed interactive spaces: teammates act together while sitting far apart, so bodily signals that colocated players read at a glance never cross the network. We present a method for closing that gap: translating Science and Technology Studies commitments, that measurement constitutes rather than reports what it names, that classification carries consequences, and that any reading of a body is situated \cite{nelson_meeting_1996, bowker_sorting_2008, haraway_situated_1988}, into a wearable interface layer that renders heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV) as an animated halo around each player's avatar, compositionally encoding two separable channels (pulse and brightness for activation, ring coherence for variability) while withholding emotion words. We report two studies. Study 1 tested the icon's legibility online with a chance-referenced forced-choice design: 36 participants read HR at 66 percent and HRV at 58 percent accuracy from animation alone, well above the 25 percent expected by chance, with interpretations ordered as designed and workload on the low side of the scale. Study 2 implemented the acquisition, classification, and rendering architecture Study 1 left as a proposed design, and evaluated it during live cooperative play: 12 three-player teams (36 participants) completed an Overcooked-style task under a no-cue baseline, a numeric HR/HRV display, and the animated cue. Teams supported a struggling teammate 3.9 seconds faster under the Icon Cue than No Cue, matching an explicit numeric display on speed and awareness while costing significantly less in invasiveness and workload, though the numeric display was rated more useful for coordination and more trustworthy, a trade-off participants named directly. We contribute the STS-to-visual-encoding method itself, a compositional two-channel encoding and a chance-referenced legibility test as secondary novelties, and converging evidence that ambient bodily cues steady coordination across a distributed interactive space at a lower privacy cost than explicit disclosure.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}
Networked multiplayer games are among the largest settings where people cooperate with others they cannot see. Strangers and friends assemble into teams, divide roles, and steer shared outcomes through fast rounds of coordination, held together by communication whose quality tracks performance: communication volume and structure separate strong teams from weak ones \cite{bisberg_communication_2025}, and even a signal as thin as a map ping measurably shifts a competitive match \cite{leavitt_ping_2016}.

One signal that teammates in a shared room read without effort never survives the network. In person, people register a partner's quickened breath, restless posture, and rising tension continuously and prereflectively, folding that reading into how they speak and act \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}. Distributed play strips this away: a remote partner must infer effort, composure, and withdrawal from text, voice, and avatar motion alone, and removing face to face cues measurably makes collaborators judge one another worse than when a representation of the partner's bodily state is restored \cite{tan_role_2014}. A teammate who has gone quiet may be concentrating, overloaded, or withdrawing, and the three call for opposite responses; absent any read on the body, players guess, and wrong guesses accumulate into misattributed blame, mistimed help, and coordination that unravels under pressure. This paper asks how to return a usable trace of that missing bodily layer to distributed teammates.

Sending physiological data across a network is not new: heart rate has been carried into chat to raise empathy \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, into shared displays to deepen social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, and into cooperative and virtual games to strengthen felt bonds \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}. Two limits recur: a long affective computing tradition treats a biosignal as evidence for an underlying emotion to be detected and named \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, fixing a single interpretation onto a body whose signals are notoriously ambiguous, and studies reporting warmer feelings between partners seldom test whether a viewer can actually read the state on display. Legibility stays largely unmeasured.

We take a different route, shaped by Science and Technology Studies. If measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears merely to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, if the categories an interface imposes carry consequences of their own \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and if every reading of a body belongs to a particular viewer and moment \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, then an interface that stamps a teammate's heartbeat with the word calm or panic overreaches. Our display withholds such verdicts, rendering Apple Watch heart rate as pulse rate and brightness, and heart rate variability as the coherence of a ring, shown as a halo around each teammate's avatar (Figure~\ref{fig:teaser}): graded, deliberately open cues a teammate weighs against the unfolding match rather than reads off as fact \cite{suchman_plans_1987, wakkary_things_2021}. The artifact is built to make an argument, not a diagnosis.

We first evaluated whether that argument is even readable. In an online study, 36 participants viewed all sixteen combinations of four heart rate states and four variability states and reported what each conveyed, recovering heart rate at 66 percent and variability at 58 percent from animation alone, well past the 25 percent expected from guessing, with interpretations ordered as intended and workload on the low side of the scale; several drew boundaries around exposing bodily data to teammates, a concern we take up directly. That study answers whether the icon is readable in isolation, not whether reading it changes what teammates do together, so we conducted a second study carrying the validated icon system into live cooperative play. Study 2 implements the architecture Study 1 left unbuilt, embeds it in an Overcooked-style \cite{carroll_utility_2019, baek_toward_2022} cooperative cooking task, and evaluates it against a no-cue baseline and an explicit numeric HR/HRV display across scripted teammate-pressure events. Twelve three-player teams supported a struggling teammate 3.9 seconds faster under the Icon Cue than No Cue, matching the Numeric Cue's speed while costing significantly less in invasiveness and workload, though the Numeric Cue was rated somewhat more useful for coordination and more trustworthy. Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods} reports this second study in full.

We contribute: a design method translating Science and Technology Studies commitments into concrete visual encoding choices that offer social cues without assigning emotions; a peripheral avatar display compositionally encoding heart rate and variability as separable visual channels rather than transmitting heart rate alone, as almost all prior shared-physiology systems do; empirical evidence, from a chance-referenced forced-choice legibility study, that this label free representation is legible, graded, and light enough in workload to be usable, together with the privacy limits players place around it; an implemented, live-multiplayer instantiation of the architecture, evaluated under no-cue, numeric-cue, and icon-cue conditions against scripted teammate-pressure events; and evidence that the ambient cue speeds teammate support as much as an explicit numeric display while costing less workload and invasiveness, alongside a legibility-versus-precision trade-off that qualifies the case for ambiguity as a design resource.

\section{Literature Review}

\subsection{Communication and Coordination in Distributed Play}
Multiplayer games reward study less as game systems than as places where scattered groups organize themselves, held together by channels that are deliberate and symbolic: messages, calls, pings \cite{bisberg_communication_2025, leavitt_ping_2016}. The involuntary bodily signals that regulate teamwork in a shared room, the ones a partner registers before either speaks \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, reach the game through no channel at all. This section traces how prior work has tried to carry the body across a network, and where each attempt stops short of the reading a teammate needs.

\subsection{Ambient Displays for Shared and Distributed Interactive Spaces}
A second, closely related tradition treats team awareness as a peripheral display glanced at without breaking focus: ambient file-activity signals \cite{morrisonsmith_ambiteam_2021}, shared smartwatch biosignals \cite{liu_animo_2019}, and proposed avatar-biosignal availability indicators in social VR \cite{elali_social_2023} all share the premise that a shared or distributed space, not only an individual's screen, is the right unit of design. Study 2's own prototype already spans more than one display in a narrower sense: three clients each render their own screen, but every screen shows the same synchronized halo state, a small multi-display environment with no single authoritative view.

\subsection{Reading Physiology as Emotion, and Sharing It Between People}
One tradition treats the body as a store of emotion to be recovered by computation. Affective computing set the agenda of sensing a signal and inferring the feeling behind it \cite{picard_affective_1997}, and play research applied it directly, mapping cardiac, muscular, and electrodermal activity onto models of a player's emotional state \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007, ravaja_phasic_2006} and adjusting game difficulty from those readings \cite{nacke_biofeedback_2011}, a lineage broad enough to have its own systematic reviews \cite{navarro_biofeedback_2021}. Surveys record both its reach and its persistent snag \cite{gan_human-computer_2022, robinson_lets_2020}: one elevated heart rate accompanies fear, exertion, and delight alike, so a system that prints a single emotion word onto it throws away the ambiguity the body carries, an interpretive move our design declines. A related strand uses heart rate not to name a feeling but to drive mechanics directly, rewarding elevated exertion with in-game power ups \cite{ketcheson_designing_2015}; this treats the signal as a control input rather than a cue to interpret, the reading problem our work addresses instead.

A second line sends the signal to another person rather than to a classifier: heart rate has been shared to build social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, folded into mobile chat to lift empathy and awareness \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, and visualized during remote collaboration, raising group cohesion and perceived warmth over voice alone \cite{tan_role_2014}. In play, coupled and visualized biosignals have deepened the bond between partners in cooperative and virtual settings \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}, consistent with evidence that physiological alignment tracks social closeness \cite{dikker_brain--brain_2017}. Shared cardiac signals have also entered more adversarial play, where visible biosignals become a strategic resource players read to infer a hidden role \cite{fang_cardiac_2022}, extending the sharing tradition beyond cooperation but leaving the same gap: readers act on the signal without a check that they identify it correctly. This work shows convincingly that a shared bodily signal changes how people relate; it seldom asks whether a viewer can name the state on show, as distinct from feeling closer to its owner, and it seldom removes the emotional framing to check that the representation still communicates. Legibility, the precondition for acting on a cue mid match, is rarely what these studies measure.

\subsection{Situated Interpretation and a Science and Technology Studies Stance}
Where the first tradition locates meaning in the signal and the second in the bond, a third locates it in the moment of use: situated and embodied accounts hold that action is read against an unfolding context rather than decoded from a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, and design research treats an artifact as a way to pose that question rather than close it \cite{wakkary_things_2021}. Science and Technology Studies sharpens the stakes for anything that measures a body: instruments help produce the phenomena they seem only to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, classifications carry consequences for those they sort \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and no reading of a body is a view from nowhere \cite{haraway_situated_1988} — together arguing against an interface that resolves a teammate's heartbeat into a verdict, and for one that offers a graded, open cue interpreted in place.

\subsection{Positioning the Present Work}
The closest prior art is Howell et al.'s ambiguous social display of skin conductance \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}, which already withholds emotion labels and treats interpretive openness as a design resource, the same move this paper makes. We differ from it, and the wider literature, in three ways rather than in the ambiguity commitment itself. First, where shared physiology systems, Howell et al.'s included, almost always transmit a single signal, we add heart rate variability as a second, separately mapped visual dimension, because the two indices carry different information: rate reflects momentary activation, variability reflects the autonomic regulation underneath it \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}. That compositional choice makes the halo a small multivariate glyph, mapping each variable onto its own visual attribute so a viewer can read either without the other overwriting it. Second, where ambiguous displays are typically evaluated through open-ended qualitative interpretation, we quantify legibility with forced-choice accuracy against a fixed chance level, reporting how much of the intended state a label-free display still communicates rather than only whether it prompts interpretation. Third, where earlier displays are judged by the connection they produce, we judge ours by the reading it supports, testing legibility before asking what it does socially. We do not claim ambiguity itself as new; we claim a compositional two-channel encoding and a chance-referenced legibility test as the specific extensions this paper adds. Table~\ref{tab:related} sets the contrast out in full.

\label{tab:related}
\begin{tabular}{@{}l p{2.1cm} p{2.8cm} p{1.1cm} p{1.5cm}@{}}

\section{Study 1: Icon Legibility Validation}
\label{sec:study1-methods}
\noindent
This paper reports two studies. Study 1, described in this section, used a research through design methodology with two phases: in the design phase, we derived the icon system from Science and Technology Studies and built the single-avatar browser stimuli that render it; in the evaluation phase, we tested the resulting visual language for legibility and interpretation in a controlled online study. Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) then implements the same icon system as a live multiplayer prototype and evaluates it during cooperative play.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
\label{sec:study1-rqh}
We addressed three research questions.
\begin{description}
    \item[RQ1] Can players identify a teammate's heart rate state and heart rate variability state from the animated icon alone, without numbers or emotion words?
    \item[RQ2] Do the two visual channels carry the graded social meaning they were designed to carry, that is, does activation read as urgency and arousal, and does declining ring coherence read as lower stability and a stronger call for support?
    \item[RQ3] Is the display clear and light enough in workload to be usable during cooperative play?
\end{description}

We set five hypotheses.
\begin{description}
    \item[H1] Identification accuracy for both the heart rate state and the variability state exceeds the 25 percent chance level of a choice among four options.
    \item[H2] Perceived urgency and arousal rise monotonically with the heart rate state.
    \item[H3] Perceived stability falls as ring coherence declines across the variability states.
    \item[H4] The intention to offer support rises as ring coherence declines.
    \item[H5] The display registers low workload on the Task Load Index while returning high clarity and coordination usefulness.
\end{description}

\subsection{Design Rationale}
Rendering a teammate's cardiovascular state at all presumes that state is worth showing. Interoception, the continuous and largely prereflective sense of one's own physiological condition, is how a person normally accesses that information \cite{craig_how_2002}; the display's task is to give a second party partial access to a process the wearer otherwise senses alone. Three STS commitments fixed how we did that. Because measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, we classified heart rate and variability relative to each player's own baseline rather than an absolute clinical scale. Because the categories an interface installs carry consequences and harden into infrastructure \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, we used a small set of relative visual states and no named emotion. Because any reading of a body is situated and partial \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, and meaning forms in place rather than inside a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, we left the cues open for interpretation against the surrounding play, following evidence that one bodily signal supports many readings depending on context \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}. We pursued the design as inquiry through research creation \cite{amos_kyriaki_2015} and speculative design \cite{wakkary_things_2021}, treating the body as a resource for interface design rather than only a source of data to be extracted \cite{dauden_roquet_body_2020}.

\subsection{Icon System and Stimuli}
The stimulus set was the icon system itself. Heart rate, an index of momentary cardiovascular activation, controlled pulse speed, glow, and halo scale. Heart rate variability, a marker of autonomic regulation estimated from the interval between beats \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007, shaffer_overview_2017}, controlled the coherence of a surrounding ring, from a smooth continuous halo to a broken, jittering one. Each channel held four states defined relative to baseline, producing 16 combinations by crossing four heart rate states with four variability states. We rendered each state as a looping five-to-seven-second animation around a single avatar. Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, a participant who had learned each channel could read a combination not seen before, and no state carried an emotion word.

Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification} defines the four heart-rate states and four HRV states used as prototype design states rather than medical diagnoses, with HRV expressed as a percentage of baseline HRV using an RMSSD-style design logic.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Heart-rate and heart-rate-variability stimulus classification used in the prototype. Thresholds were defined relative to each player's own baseline and were used to structure the simulated visual states rather than to provide medical, clinical, or emotional diagnoses.}
\label{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification}
\scriptsize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.95}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.17\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{State} &
\textbf{Physiological meaning} &
\textbf{Prototype threshold} &
\textbf{Icon effect} \\
\midrule
\textbf{HR1: Resting / Low Activation} &
Calm or low cardiovascular activation &
Baseline to $+5$ bpm, or $\leq 10\%$ above baseline &
Slow halo pulse and soft glow \\
\textbf{HR2: Focused Activation} &
Engaged, alert, and mildly activated &
$+6$ to $+15$ bpm, or $11$ to $25\%$ above baseline &
Medium pulse and clearer glow \\
\textbf{HR3: High Activation} &
Intense engagement, pressure, or effort &
$+16$ to $+30$ bpm, or $26$ to $45\%$ above baseline &
Fast pulse and stronger glow \\
\textbf{HR4: Overdrive Activation} &
Very high activation and possible overload &
More than $+30$ bpm, or more than $45\%$ above baseline &
Rapid pulse, sharp glow, and stronger expansion \\
\midrule
\textbf{V1: High Coherence} &
High variability and flexible regulation &
$\geq 110\%$ of baseline HRV &
Smooth continuous ring, wide breathing halo, and stable motion \\
\textbf{V2: Stable Variability} &
Normal or baseline variability &
$90$ to $109\%$ of baseline HRV &
Clean dotted ring and regular halo motion \\
\textbf{V3: Reduced Variability} &
Lower flexibility and possible physiological strain &
$60$ to $89\%$ of baseline HRV &
Denser segmented ring with a slight wobble \\
\textbf{V4: Suppressed / Fragmented Variability} &
Very low variability and reduced regulatory stability &
Less than $60\%$ of baseline HRV &
Broken ring, visible jitter, and irregular opacity \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification} summarizes the baseline-relative HR and HRV classifications used to generate the sixteen animated stimulus conditions. We label the four heart rate states HR1 to HR4 and the four variability states V1 to V4 throughout; these condition labels are distinct from the H1 to H5 hypotheses stated in Section~\ref{sec:study1-rqh}.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{Icon.jpg}
  \caption{The icon design evolves through theoretical perspectives from Science and Technology Studies (STS), transforming an abstract sensing field into a human avatar surrounded by a dynamic halo. The final icon visualizes physiological signals as an ambiguous social cue, where halo brightness, pulse, density, and stability vary with heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV).}
  \Description{A visual design figure showing variations in avatar shapes and layered halo structures used to represent changes in heart rate and heart-rate variability.}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Study Design}
We used a within participants design in which every participant viewed all 16 states, removing between-person variation from the central comparisons, matching recent within-subjects recognition tests of icon sets in HCI \cite{schafer_shape_2023} and recent work evaluating heart-rate-driven displays through player-facing studies \cite{tu_biofeedback_2025}. We administered it online in two phases: an open phase presenting 4 states per participant with all labels hidden, and a structured phase presenting all 16 states in randomised order, 20 rating trials per participant (144 open-phase and 576 structured-phase trials across the sample), followed by one closing questionnaire. The design tests absolute legibility against a fixed chance level rather than comparative legibility against an alternative encoding, following ISO 9186 symbol-comprehension convention \cite{iso_9186_2014}. We did not include a numeric or emotion-labeled comparison arm; Section~\ref{sec:limitations} returns to what that omission does and does not let us claim, and Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods} reports a follow-up study making exactly that comparison.

\subsection{Participants, Apparatus, and Procedure}
We recruited thirty six adults through the author's university subject pool and personal network; participation was voluntary and uncompensated, and all recruited participants completed the study. Twenty one were aged 18-24, twelve were 25-34, two were 35-44, and one was 45-54; most reported regular or frequent gaming experience, moderate familiarity with heart rate and variability, and no visual accessibility issues (full demographics in the supplementary materials, Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}). We ran the study in a standard web browser, presenting one avatar inside a simulated team status panel; the heart rate and variability states were synthetic, using the same animation logic the deployed interface would use, isolating the reading of the display from the demands of live play. Each session opened with consent and a demographic form, then moved through two phases: an open phase, with every label hidden, in which participants viewed each state, wrote in free text what it conveyed, and rated confidence, activation, and stability; and a structured phase, in which participants saw all 16 states in randomised order and, for each, identified the heart rate and variability states from four options apiece, rated clarity, urgency, stability, coordination usefulness, and support intention, and completed a pictorial affective rating. A closing questionnaire recorded workload, confidence, visual comfort, and five open responses on the most helpful feature, most confusing states, invasiveness, willingness to use it in cooperative play, and suggested improvements.

\subsection{Ethics Note for Reviewers}
Study 1 and Study 2 were reviewed and approved together under a single institutional research ethics board approval, obtained prior to data collection. All participants gave informed consent before beginning the session, were told they could withdraw at any time without penalty, and were assigned an anonymous participant identifier.

\subsection{Measures and Analysis}
We measured legibility as identification accuracy against the 25 percent chance level, following the forced choice recognition format used in recent icon studies \cite{schafer_shape_2023}. We assessed affective interpretation with the Self-Assessment Manikin \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, workload with three NASA Task Load Index items \cite{hart_development_1988}, and social reading with five further seven point items (clarity, urgency, stability, coordination usefulness, support intention). We fixed the sample at 36 to match comparable within-subjects icon-comprehension studies \cite{schafer_shape_2023}; a sensitivity analysis indicates this gives at least 99 percent power to detect a large effect ($d_z \geq 0.8$) and approximately 83 percent power for a medium effect ($d_z = 0.5$). Each legibility comparison tested one participant-level accuracy proportion against chance with a two sided one sample t test, reported with Cohen's $d_z$; the four comparisons under H1 were treated as individually pre-specified confirmatory tests, and even under a conservative Bonferroni correction ($\alpha=.0125$) every comparison remained significant. We tested the intended perceptual mappings with planned linear trend analyses, Holm corrected across the five planned tests, and analysed the free text interpretations and open responses with reflexive thematic analysis \cite{braun_using_2006, bowman_thematic_2023}.

\subsection{Proposed System Architecture}
What we built and evaluated for Study 1 is the single-avatar icon-visualization layer described above, driven by scripted rather than live physiological input. The acquisition and classification layers needed to drive that layer from a real wearable were, at the time of Study 1, a proposed rather than a built architecture: an Apple Watch (Series 8 or later) sampling heart rate at 1 Hz from its photoplethysmography sensor, with heart rate and heart-rate-variability samples, HRV computed as RMSSD, the root mean square of successive differences between beat-to-beat intervals, read through Apple's HealthKit over Bluetooth Low Energy, and a processing layer classifying both against each player's own resting baseline from a seated calibration period rather than an absolute clinical scale \cite{haraway_situated_1988, bowker_sorting_2008} (Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification} gives the four-state classification this layer used for each channel). Each avatar's halo was designed to be driven by only these two small classified state values rather than a raw physiological time series, a property that keeps the encoding itself portable to any rendering engine or display rather than tied to the one it was later built in, so propagating a player's state to teammates across a multiplayer session would require replicating only those two values, not a continuous stream, over a lightweight local bridge between device and game client that prior work shows is practical \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}. Study 2 (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) implements this acquisition, classification, and avatar-rendering pipeline as a working multiplayer prototype and evaluates it during live cooperative play.

\subsection{Results}
\label{sec:study1-results}
Thirty six participants produced 576 recognition trials in the structured phase and 144 free interpretations in the open phase. Each participant contributed one linear trend slope per outcome, and we tested each slope against zero with a two sided one sample t test, with Holm correction across the five planned tests. We report legibility, then the intended perceptual mappings, then performance across the full grid, then usability and workload, then the qualitative findings.

\subsubsection{Icon Legibility (H1)}
\label{sec:study1-legibility}
Before any label was shown, participants' best-match interpretations of the four unaided anchor states in the open phase averaged 46.5 percent correct against the 20 percent chance level (95 percent CI 37.0-56.0), $t(35)=5.66$, $p<.001$. In the structured phase, participants identified the heart rate state on 66.3 percent of trials (95 percent CI 58.5-74.2) and the variability state on 58.2 percent (95 percent CI 51.0-65.3), both $p<.001$ and far above the 25 percent chance level, supporting H1. Accuracy held across the range of states, with errors concentrated on neighbouring levels (HR4 read as HR3 on 27.8 percent of its trials) and no state falling below 49.3 percent. Identifying both channels correctly on the same trial, a harder sixteen-way judgement, reached 44.6 percent against a 6.25 percent chance level (95 percent CI 36.8-52.5), $t(35)=9.90$, $p<.001$. Figure~\ref{fig:legibility} reports recognition against chance with the HR and HRV confusion matrices; full statistics are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.78\textwidth]{Figure_4_Identification_Accuracy_Confusion.png}
  \caption{Identification accuracy and confusion patterns. (a) Unaided best-match accuracy for the four Phase A anchor conditions against the 20 percent chance level. (b) Mean participant accuracy for HR recognition, HRV recognition, and joint HR+HRV recognition in the structured phase, against the 25 percent and 6.25 percent chance levels. (c) HR confusion matrix and (d) HRV confusion matrix, showing the percentage of trials at each actual state that were assigned to each selected state.}
  \Description{A four-panel figure: unaided Phase A comprehension per anchor condition, Phase B recognition accuracy for HR, HRV, and joint recognition against chance lines, and HR and HRV confusion matrices showing that most errors fall on adjacent states.}
  \label{fig:legibility}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Perceived Activation, Stability, and the Call for Support (H2, H3, H4)}
\label{sec:study1-trends}
Both channels carried their intended social meaning. Each step up the heart rate state raised perceived urgency by 0.40 points on the seven-point scale (95 percent CI 0.24-0.55, $t(35)=5.17$, $p<.001$) and arousal by 0.41 points per level ($t(35)=5.33$, $p<.001$), supporting H2, though the slope was shallower than the accuracy results alone would suggest. Each step toward a more fragmented ring lowered perceived stability by 0.46 points ($t(35)=6.52$, $p<.001$, supporting H3) and raised support intention by 0.48 points per level ($t(35)=7.78$, $p<.001$, supporting H4), with rated control falling on the same axis ($t(35)=7.35$, $p<.001$). All trends survived Holm correction across the five planned tests; Figure~\ref{fig:mappings} plots both curves, with full statistics in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{Figure_5_Perceptual_Mappings.png}
  \caption{Validation of the intended perceptual mappings. (a) Perceived urgency and SAM arousal rise with heart rate state, from low activation (H1) to overdrive activation (H4). (b) Perceived stability and SAM dominance/control fall, while support intention rises, as ring coherence declines across the variability states, from high coherence (V1) to fragmented variability (V4).}
  \Description{Two line charts with confidence intervals: urgency and arousal rising across the four heart rate states, and stability and dominance falling while support intention rises across the four variability states.}
  \label{fig:mappings}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Performance Across the Full Grid}
Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, legibility could have collapsed where an active pulse met a fragmented ring. It did not: joint recognition held across all 16 combinations, with the hardest cell (33.3 percent) still more than five times above the 6.25 percent chance level, confirming participants read the two channels compositionally rather than memorising sixteen unrelated icons. Support intention rose consistently with fragmentation, from 3.00 at H1V1 to 5.06 at H4V4 (Figure~\ref{fig:conditions}).

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{Figure_6_Full_Condition_Grid.png}
  \caption{Performance across the full HR $\times$ HRV condition grid. (a) Joint recognition accuracy for all 16 combinations, against a 6.25 percent chance level; the hardest cells paired stable variability with mid-range activation, and the easiest paired coherent variability with a clearly placed activation level. (b) Mean support-intention rating for the same 16 combinations, rising with both activation and fragmentation.}
  \Description{Two heatmaps of the four heart rate states by four variability states: joint recognition accuracy, with all cells well above chance and the lowest values concentrated among stable-variability, mid-activation combinations, and mean support-intention ratings, increasing toward the high-activation, fragmented-variability corner.}
  \label{fig:conditions}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Usability, Workload, and Participant Interpretation (H5)}
The display read clearly at a moderate but sub-midpoint effort: clarity averaged 5.22 of 7, coordination usefulness 5.45, and the three Task Load Index items stayed below the scale midpoint (mental demand 3.64, effort 3.47, frustration 2.53), supporting H5, though workload sat closer to the midpoint than a minimal-effort display would, consistent with the moderate recognition accuracy above (full CIs in the supplementary materials, Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\subsubsection{How Participants Read the States}
The open responses explain the recognition scores. Participants reported reading the two channels together rather than in isolation, using pulse speed for activation and the ring for control, as the compositional design intended: ``The combination worked better than either cue alone. Pulse speed gave the intensity, and the ring showed whether it was controlled'' (P12). The hardest states were the adjacent middle levels on each scale, matching the neighbour confusions in the recognition data; as P4 put it, ``V2 and V3 were the closest pair for me. I could see that the ring changed, but not always enough to choose confidently.''

\subsubsection{Usefulness, Boundaries, and Willingness to Use}
Participants judged the display useful for cooperative play but tied that judgement to context and control, not the visual design alone; P13 said the information ``could be genuinely helpful during difficult sections of a game. It becomes invasive only if teammates or spectators can access it automatically.'' Participants asked for control over who can see their status, an optional and mutable display, and consent built into the default rather than added later, with P4 willing to accept it ``only with clear consent and an easy off switch.'' Willingness to adopt was expressed most often for cooperative and squad modes where a teammate cannot pause to explain their state.

\subsection{Discussion}
Study 1's results answer RQ1 and RQ2: the label free display is legible well above chance, and its two channels carry the graded social meaning intended. RQ3 is answered with a caveat, since workload sat closer to the scale's midpoint than a minimal-effort display would predict, tracking the moderate rather than ceiling-level accuracy. What the study cannot show, using simulated states and no comparison condition, is whether this legibility survives a live wearable feed or an explicit numeric alternative; Study 2 takes up both questions.

\section{Study 2: Live Multiplayer Evaluation of Physiological Awareness Cues}
\label{sec:study2-methods}
\noindent
To evaluate whether the architecture proposed in Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods} supports coordination during actual cooperative play, we designed a second study that keeps Study 1's visual language untouched and moves the evaluation into live cooperative gameplay, asking whether live physiological visibility changes teammate awareness, support timing, coordination behavior, workload, and privacy perception, and whether the wearable-to-interface pipeline itself operates reliably enough for live play.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
We addressed four research questions, distinct from and building on the RQ1 to RQ3 of Study 1.
\begin{description}
    \item[RQ2.1] Does a live HR/HRV avatar cue improve remote teammates' awareness of one another during cooperative gameplay?
    \item[RQ2.2] Does it improve support timing and coordination relative to no cue and to a numeric physiological display?
    \item[RQ2.3] Does an ambient animated cue feel less invasive and more usable than explicit numeric disclosure?
    \item[RQ2.4] Does the implemented wearable-to-interface pipeline operate reliably enough for live cooperative play?
\end{description}

We set six hypotheses, distinct from and building on the H1 to H5 of Study 1.
\begin{description}
    \item[H2.1 (Awareness)] Participants report higher teammate awareness in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition.
    \item[H2.2 (Coordination usefulness)] Participants rate the Icon Cue condition as more useful for coordination than both the No Cue and Numeric Cue conditions.
    \item[H2.3 (Support response)] Teams respond faster to scripted teammate-pressure events in the Icon Cue condition than in the No Cue condition.
    \item[H2.4 (Privacy/invasiveness)] The Numeric Cue condition is rated as more invasive than the Icon Cue condition.
    \item[H2.5 (Workload)] The Icon Cue condition does not produce higher workload than the Numeric Cue condition and remains at or below the midpoint of the workload scale.
    \item[H2.6 (Communication pattern)] The Icon Cue condition reduces general uncertainty and status-check communication while increasing targeted support behavior.
\end{description}

\subsection{Gameplay Task and Physiological Apparatus}
The gameplay task is modeled on Overcooked-style cooperative cooking \cite{team17_overcooked_2018}, in which players deliver orders by collecting, preparing, and cooking ingredients, then plating and delivering the dish before time expires, splitting tasks and adapting roles under time pressure \cite{carroll_utility_2019, humancompatibleai_overcookedai}, a genre that has supported human-AI coordination research and multiplayer studies of role division \cite{baek_toward_2022}. We adapted this into a custom cooperative cooking task built in Unity rather than using copyrighted commercial levels, preserving the core action chain of ingredient collection, preparation, reactor cooking, plating, and delivery, so team performance, role division, and teammate overload remain measurable and comparable across conditions.

Study 2 implements the physiological pipeline Study 1 left as a proposed architecture (Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods}): each participant wore an Apple Watch (Series 8 or later), whose photoplethysmography sensor and HealthKit-derived heart-rate and RMSSD heart-rate-variability samples reached the game client over Bluetooth Low Energy through a lightweight local bridge, with all processing kept on the client device rather than a remote server. Before gameplay, each participant completed a three-minute seated baseline calibration to set personal resting HR and HRV. During gameplay, live values were classified relative to each participant's own baseline into the same four HR and four HRV states validated in Study 1 (Table~\ref{tab:hr-hrv-stimulus-classification}); HR was sampled at 1 Hz, so classification updated once per second, but the visible cue changed only once a new state held across several consecutive samples, a short debounce that filtered single-sample noise without adding perceptible lag, to limit flicker. Because each player's state was reduced to two small classified values rather than a raw stream, a viewing teammate's client never received the wearer's physiological time series directly: it received only that player's current state, replicated over the same multiplayer network channel already carrying position and action data, and rendered the halo locally. The system logged raw physiological values, baselines, classified and displayed states, timestamps, missing-data events, and sensor-to-interface latency, which together speak to RQ2.4.

\subsection{Participants and Team Size}
Study 2's 36 participants were the same 36 individuals who took part in Study 1, drawn from the same university subject pool and personal network, invited back and organized into 12 teams of three players in soft Prep, Cook, and Runner roles rather than 9 teams of four, since team-size research finds that additional members increase coordination overhead \cite{hoegl_smaller_2005, straub_cost_2023} and 12 teams give more independent team-level units for the analyses below than 9 would. Reusing Study 1's sample rather than recruiting a fresh one means the two studies are not fully independent evidence: every Study 2 participant had already completed Study 1's structured phase, exposed to all 16 HR/HRV combinations under labels-hidden, forced-choice conditions, so they arrived at the Icon Cue condition with more prior exposure than a first-time viewer would have; Section~\ref{sec:limitations} returns to what this means for interpreting Study 2's results. Roles were introduced during training but remained flexible: players could switch tasks mid-mission, logged as strategy-change events rather than protocol violations. Participation was voluntary and uncompensated in both studies, and all 36 participants completed all three mission blocks, so no team contributed incomplete data. The sample skewed young (50 percent aged 18 to 24) and toward regular or frequent gaming experience, with substantial cooperative-game experience generally but more mixed exposure to Overcooked-style titles specifically, mixed prior wearable use and familiarity with HR/HRV concepts, and moderate baseline comfort sharing physiological data ($M=4.47$ of 7, $SD=0.74$); full demographic breakdowns by category are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\subsection{Interface Conditions and Counterbalancing}
Each team completed three seven-minute missions, one under each of three interface conditions: a No Cue baseline showing only the standard game interface; a Numeric Cue condition showing each teammate's current HR in bpm and HRV in ms as live numbers beneath their role label, an upper bound on explicit disclosure; and an Icon Cue condition showing the animated HR/HRV avatar cue validated in Study 1 (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-conditions}). Condition order was counterbalanced with a Latin-square schedule across three groups of four teams, so every condition appeared in every serial position, and the three mission layouts below were rotated across orders so no condition was paired systematically with an easier or harder layout: Group A played No Cue on the Shared Congestion layout, Numeric Cue on the Divided Pass layout, and Icon Cue on the Bottleneck Route layout, while Groups B and C rotated the same pairings into the other two orders; the full schedule is in the supplementary materials.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \begin{subfigure}[b]{0.325\textwidth}
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_1_Teaser_NoCue.png}
    \caption{No Cue}
    \label{fig:s2-conditions-nocue}
  \end{subfigure}
  \hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[b]{0.325\textwidth}
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_1_Teaser_NumericCue.png}
    \caption{Numeric Cue}
    \label{fig:s2-conditions-numeric}
  \end{subfigure}
  \hfill
  \begin{subfigure}[b]{0.325\textwidth}
    \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_1_Teaser_IconCue.png}
    \caption{Icon Cue}
    \label{fig:s2-conditions-icon}
  \end{subfigure}
  \caption{The three Study 2 interface conditions, captured in the custom Unity cooperative-cooking prototype. (a) No Cue: the standard game interface, with no physiological information for any teammate. (b) Numeric Cue: an explicit readout of each teammate's current HR (bpm) and HRV (ms) beneath their role label. (c) Icon Cue: the same three teammates rendered instead as a pulsing, glowing halo around each role icon, encoding HR as pulse and glow and HRV as ring coherence, with no numbers or emotion words shown. All three conditions ran the same cooperative cooking task, with players filling the Prep, Cook, and Runner roles shown at the bottom of the screen.}
  \Description{Three side-by-side screenshots of the same Overcooked-style cooperative cooking game, viewed from a top-down angle with a kitchen on the right and a dining area on the left. The first screenshot shows a plain interface with no physiological display. The second shows a status bar naming the Prep, Cook, and Runner players with explicit heart rate in beats per minute and heart rate variability in milliseconds listed under each. The third replaces those numbers with glowing circular halos around each player's role icon, varying in brightness and pulse without any numeric or text labels.}
  \label{fig:s2-conditions}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Mission Layouts, Scripted Pressure Events, and Procedure}
The three mission layouts reproduce common Overcooked-style cooperation patterns \cite{team17_overcooked_2018, carroll_utility_2019, baek_toward_2022}: a shared congestion kitchen with clustered resources and collision risk, a divided pass kitchen requiring mandatory item passing between zones, and a bottleneck-route kitchen where solo routing is inefficient and temporary support pays off. Each seven-minute mission also included three scripted pressure events, controlled coordination probes rather than random obstacles, each leaving the other two players holding incomplete information about it: an Ingredient Bottleneck around 2:00, in which two active orders suddenly required the same ingredient, overloading the Prep player and measured by support response time from event onset; a Pot Overload around 4:30, in which two reactor pots became active close together, overloading the Cook player and measured by recovery time and burned-dish prevention; and a Service Rush in the mission's final phase, in which completed dishes, dirty plates, and delivery tasks accumulated at once, overloading the Runner player and measured by whether an appropriate support action followed within ten seconds. Each session opened with consent, a demographic questionnaire, wearable setup, baseline calibration, and a tutorial, then three mission blocks, one per condition, each followed by a post-condition questionnaire, and closed with a final comparison questionnaire ranking the three conditions on usefulness, invasiveness, trust, confusion, and desired visibility control.

\subsection{Measures and Analysis Plan}
The primary behavioral outcome was support response time, the seconds from a pressure event's onset to the first relevant support action by another teammate. Secondary behavioral measures, logged automatically, included orders delivered, mission score, errors, idle time, collision count, appropriate and false support actions, and recovery time; full operational definitions are in the supplementary materials. Team communication was coded post hoc into eight categories to test whether the cue shifts the balance between general uncertainty and targeted support (H2.6). After each condition, participants completed seven-point items on teammate awareness, coordination usefulness, and trust; the NASA Task Load Index \cite{hart_development_1988}; the Self-Assessment Manikin \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, as in Study 1; selected Game Experience Questionnaire dimensions \cite{ijsselsteijn_game_2013}; and custom items on comfort, invasiveness, and desired visibility control, with related items combined into composite constructs where they held together statistically. Behavioral outcomes were analyzed at the team-by-condition level with repeated-measures ANOVA and planned contrasts (Icon vs.\ No Cue, Icon vs.\ Numeric, Numeric vs.\ No Cue), the primary contrast being Icon vs.\ No Cue on support response time; questionnaire ratings were analyzed at the participant-by-condition level; coded communication frequencies were compared at the team-by-condition level; and physiological and interface logs were summarized descriptively for RQ2.4.

\subsection{Ethics Note for Reviewers}
Study 2 was conducted under the same ethics board approval as Study 1 (Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods}). All participants gave informed consent and were told HR and HRV data were collected for research and interface-visualization purposes only, not medical diagnosis or emotional classification. Participants were assigned anonymous identifiers, physiological data were stored separately from identifying information, and participants could withdraw or stop physiological sharing at any time without penalty.

\subsection{Results}
\label{sec:study2-results}
Study 2 achieved its planned sample without attrition: all 36 recruited participants, organized as 12 teams of three, completed all three mission blocks under all three interface conditions, yielding 36 team-missions, 108 scripted pressure events, 864 physiological log entries, and 488 coded communication utterances. Full per-outcome statistics beyond those reported below are in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}).

\subsubsection{Sample, Data Completeness, and System Reliability (RQ2.4)}
\label{sec:s2-reliability}
Participants' mean baseline resting HR was 69.7 bpm ($SD=6.9$) and mean baseline HRV was 52.6 ms RMSSD ($SD=8.7$). Sensor-to-interface latency averaged 180 ms under No Cue and rose to 223 to 247 ms once cue state was also replicated and rendered on teammates' clients, well under a second and not accumulating across the mission (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}A). Missing-data episodes were rare (3.5-4.5 percent of samples) and dropouts brief in every condition (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}B, C). The displayed state matched the classified state on over 96 percent of samples, confirming the persistence rule (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}) controlled flicker without decoupling display from signal; classified states covered the full range, with participants spending 13.6, 63.6, 22.7, and 0.1 percent of samples in HR1-4 and 0.6, 14.8, 63.3, and 21.3 percent in V1-4 (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-reliability}E). Together, these answer RQ2.4: the pipeline operated reliably across 12 teams and three conditions.

\subsubsection{Support Response Time and Behavioral Coordination (H2.3)}
\label{sec:s2-behavioral}
H2.3 predicted faster support responses under the Icon Cue than No Cue; this was Study 2's primary planned contrast, and it was supported. Teams took 15.3 seconds to produce the first relevant support action after a pressure event's onset under No Cue, 12.5 seconds under Numeric Cue, and 11.4 seconds under Icon Cue (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}A), the Icon Cue 3.90 seconds faster than No Cue ($d_z=-1.46$) and numerically, but not significantly, faster than the Numeric Cue. This held across all three scripted event types (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}A), and recovery time showed the same ordering, 29.0s under No Cue to 23.2s under Icon Cue ($d_z=-1.53$; Figure~\ref{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}B).

The benefit extended to whether support was well targeted, not merely faster: teams logged more appropriate support actions under both cue conditions than No Cue (2.25 to 3.17/3.42) while false support actions did not differ (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-performance}). One asymmetry qualifies this: on mission score, orders delivered, and error count, the Numeric Cue numerically outperformed the Icon Cue, reaching significance for mission score ($d_z=-0.73$) — a team with exact numbers still out-scored a team reading the halo on the mission's aggregate score, a nuance Section~\ref{sec:discussion} returns to.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.72\textwidth]{Figure_5_Pressure_Event_Breakdown_Clean.png}
  \caption{Support response time (A) and recovery time (B) by interface condition, broken down by the three scripted pressure-event types. The benefit of a visible cue held across all three events rather than concentrating in one.}
  \Description{Two panels of connected-dot plots, one for support response time and one for recovery time, each showing three lines (No Cue, Numeric Cue, Icon Cue) across the Ingredient Bottleneck, Pot Overload, and Service Rush pressure events; both cue conditions sit below the No Cue line at every event.}
  \label{fig:s2-pressure-breakdown}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.72\textwidth]{Figure_6_Team_Performance_Outcomes.png}
  \caption{Team performance outcomes by interface condition: (A) orders delivered, (B) mission score, (C) error count (late + burned + wrong orders), (D) appropriate support actions. Gray lines show individual teams; black markers show condition means $\pm$ 95\% CI.}
  \Description{A four-panel figure of team-level performance measures across the three interface conditions, with orders delivered and mission score rising under both cue conditions relative to No Cue, error count falling, and appropriate support actions rising, most under the Icon Cue.}
  \label{fig:s2-performance}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Teammate Awareness, Coordination Usefulness, Workload, and Privacy (H2.1, H2.2, H2.4, H2.5)}
\label{sec:s2-awareness}
We combined related seven-point items into constructs: teammate awareness ($\alpha=.67$), coordination usefulness ($\alpha=.77$), and trust ($\alpha=.66$). H2.1 was supported with a large effect: awareness rose from 4.21 under No Cue to 5.40 under Icon Cue ($d_z=1.63$), statistically indistinguishable from the Numeric Cue (5.41; $d_z=-0.01$) — the label-free halo made a teammate's state exactly as noticeable as an explicit readout without exposing the numbers. H2.2 was only partially supported: coordination usefulness rose sharply from No Cue to Icon Cue ($d_z=1.93$), but the Numeric Cue was rated \emph{more} useful (5.49 vs.\ 5.27, $d_z=-0.41$), the reverse of the predicted direction, echoing the mission-score asymmetry above and a trade-off several participants named directly (Section~\ref{sec:s2-qualitative}); trust followed the same shape ($d_z=-0.56$). Figure~\ref{fig:s2-questionnaire} plots these constructs.

\label{sec:s2-workload}
H2.5 was supported: the four NASA-TLX items did not cohere into a single reliable index ($\alpha=.13$), so we report them individually; on every item the Icon Cue's mean stayed at or below the scale midpoint and did not differ from No Cue, while the Numeric Cue significantly raised mental demand, temporal demand, and effort, and a four-item descriptive mean confirmed the Icon Cue cost significantly less ($d_z=-0.51$). The Numeric Cue also gave players a stronger sense of control ($d_z=-0.46$); the Game Experience Questionnaire composite rose from No Cue to both cue conditions without differing between them.

\label{sec:s2-privacy}
H2.4 was strongly supported: the Icon Cue was rated significantly less invasive than the Numeric Cue ($d_z=-1.22$), though still more invasive than No Cue ($d_z=1.03$). Comfort sharing physiological data showed the mirror pattern ($d_z=0.44$), and the desire for visibility control and an immediate off switch rose sharply once any physiological data was shown, regardless of form: players wanted control over \emph{any} bodily disclosure. Figure~\ref{fig:s2-questionnaire} plots the remaining constructs.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{Figure_7_Post_Condition_Questionnaire_Outcomes.png}
  \caption{Post-condition questionnaire outcomes by interface condition (participant-level means $\pm$ 95\% CI; light lines show individual participants). Exact means and inferential tests for all constructs are reported in the text and supplementary materials.}
  \Description{A six-panel figure of post-condition questionnaire ratings by interface condition, covering teammate awareness, coordination usefulness, trust, workload, invasiveness, and player experience; trust, player experience, and awareness rise under both cue conditions relative to No Cue, invasiveness rises most under Numeric Cue, and workload rises only under Numeric Cue.}
  \label{fig:s2-questionnaire}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Communication Behavior (H2.6)}
\label{sec:s2-communication}
H2.6 was partially supported. Coders classified 488 utterances across the 36 team-missions into eight categories (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-communication}). Uncertainty statements and help requests fell sharply once any cue was present ($p<.001$ for both), but not further under the Icon Cue than the Numeric Cue; status-check utterances did not differ reliably. Support offers and strategy-change utterances rose specifically under the Icon Cue, ahead of both other conditions ($p<.001$ throughout), the clearest communication-level signature of the ambient design. References to the displayed cue were condition-locked to their matching condition, and task-instruction utterances, the most frequent code overall, fell steadily as ambient awareness increased.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.68\textwidth]{Figure_8_Communication_Behavior_Fixed.png}
  \caption{Coded communication rate per team-mission by interface condition. Support offers and strategy-change talk rise specifically under the Icon Cue; uncertainty statements and help requests fall under both cue conditions relative to No Cue; physiological and numeric references are condition-locked to the Icon Cue and Numeric Cue respectively.}
  \Description{A grouped horizontal bar chart of coded communication rates across seven categories and three interface conditions, showing support offers highest under Icon Cue, uncertainty statements and help requests lowest under both cue conditions, and physiological/numeric references appearing only in their matching condition.}
  \label{fig:s2-communication}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{Figure_9_System_Reliability_Clean.png}
  \caption{System reliability across the three interface conditions: sensor-latency distribution (A), missing-data percentage (B), dropout duration (C), and HR/HRV state-change counts (E). Display-state agreement and baseline HR/HRV are reported in the text.}
  \Description{A multi-panel figure of system reliability measures, showing sensor latency rising slightly under the two cue conditions relative to No Cue, missing-data percentage and dropout duration low and similar across conditions, and HR/HRV state-change counts comparable across conditions.}
  \label{fig:s2-reliability}
\end{figure*}

\subsubsection{Final Preferences and Qualitative Findings}
\label{sec:s2-qualitative}
Participants ranked the three conditions on five criteria at session's end (Figure~\ref{fig:s2-preferences}). The Icon Cue was named most useful for coordination (50 percent) and preferred for real cooperative play (53 percent); the Numeric Cue was named easiest to understand and most trustworthy slightly more often, mirroring Section~\ref{sec:s2-awareness}; and No Cue was named least invasive by a wide majority (78 percent), consistent with Section~\ref{sec:s2-privacy}.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.68\textwidth]{Figure_10_Final_Condition_Preference_Final.png}
  \caption{Participants' final condition preferences by criterion. Each row sums to 100 percent of participants' first-choice selections.}
  \Description{A stacked horizontal bar chart with five rows, one per ranking criterion (most useful, easiest to understand, least invasive, most trustworthy, preferred for real play), each showing the percentage of participants who selected No Cue, Numeric Cue, or Icon Cue as their first choice; No Cue dominates least invasive, while Icon Cue leads most useful and preferred for real play, and Numeric Cue leads easiest to understand and most trustworthy by a narrow margin.}
  \label{fig:s2-preferences}
\end{figure*}

We coded the nine open-ended closing-interview questions using the theme labels applied during data collection; the full breakdown is in the supplementary materials (Section~\ref{sec:data-availability}). Participants frequently framed the Icon Cue and Numeric Cue as offering different things rather than one beating the other, a trade-off between glanceability and precision: ``The icon felt more like part of the game; the numbers felt like a measurement tool. The trade-off was speed versus precision'' (P042). Several described the cue changing how they interpreted a quiet teammate specifically, the scenario the design was built for. Privacy surfaced unprompted and consistently, with participants asking for opt-in sharing per match, an immediate off switch, and separate visibility permissions for the icon and the exact numbers. These qualitative accounts converge with the quantitative results: an ambient, label-free cue delivered most of the coordination benefit of explicit disclosure while asking less of players' attention and privacy.

\subsection{Discussion}
Study 2's results answer RQ2.1 through RQ2.3: a live HR/HRV avatar cue improves support timing and coordination at lower workload and invasiveness than an explicit numeric display, though not at a uniform advantage, since the Numeric Cue matched or beat the Icon Cue on coordination usefulness, trust, and mission score. RQ2.4 is also answered affirmatively: the pipeline operated reliably across all three conditions. Section~\ref{sec:discussion} and Section~\ref{sec:limitations} take up what this mixed result means.

\FloatBarrier

\section{Overall Discussion}
\label{sec:discussion}

Study 1 asked whether a teammate's body could be made readable at a distance without naming a feeling; Study 2 asked whether that readability changes what a team does together in live play. Both answer yes, largely in the predicted direction, with one recurring qualification: the ambient encoding does not beat an explicit numeric alternative on every measure.

\subsection{Legibility Without Emotional Labels, as a Designed Resource}
The central Study 1 result is that an interface can withhold every emotion word and still be read, well above chance, with readings ordered by the underlying states across all five planned tests. This separates two things the shared physiology literature tends to bundle: systems that name an emotion assume the naming is warranted \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, and systems that report warmer feeling between partners rarely check that the viewer can identify the state on show \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, hirsch_my_2023, hassan_augmenting_2024}. Prior ambiguous biosignal displays, closest among them Howell et al.'s skin-conductance work \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}, established that withholding a label does not prevent interpretation; our contribution is narrower and more quantitative, a chance-referenced identification rate for a graded rather than categorical label-free display. That choice was not incidental: because measurement helps constitute what it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, because the categories an interface fixes carry consequences \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and because any reading of a body is situated \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, the interface offered graded cues to be interpreted in place rather than settled verdicts, and participants valued that it showed a pattern rather than a diagnosis. We also encoded two physiologically distinct channels, pulse and brightness against ring coherence \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}, rather than heart rate alone as almost all prior work does; performance held across all sixteen combinations, evidence that more than one bodily signal can be carried at once when each maps to a visual variable a viewer can isolate.

\subsection{From Reading a State to Coordinating}
A display meant for coordination must do more than convey a state; it has to move a teammate toward a response, and the variability channel did exactly that in Study 1: as ring coherence broke down, support intention rose monotonically across the grid. Study 2 tested whether that intention converts into faster support during live play, and it did: teams supported a struggling teammate significantly faster under the Icon Cue than No Cue, a large effect across all three pressure-event types, with more appropriate support actions and no more false ones, extending the connection-oriented framing of earlier heart rate sharing systems \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017, janssen_intimate_2010, jarvela_augmented_2021} into a demonstrated behavioral effect.

The three-condition design also answered a comparison Study 1's single-arm design could not make: whether withholding numeric values costs or gains anything relative to explicit disclosure. The answer is not a clean win for ambiguity. On support response time and teammate awareness, the Icon Cue matched the Numeric Cue, and beat it outright on workload and perceived invasiveness, the strongest form the ambiguity argument could take. But on coordination usefulness, trust, and mission score, the Numeric Cue performed significantly better, a trade-off between glanceability and precision participants named directly (Section~\ref{sec:s2-qualitative}): the icon was read faster and with less friction, but exact numbers gave players something concrete to plan around. A design wanting the numeric condition's edge without its privacy cost would need to combine the two, keeping the ambient halo as the default and surfacing exact values on request.

\subsection{Beyond a Personal Screen: Portability to Shared and Distributed Surfaces}
Every session here rendered the halo on an individual player's screen, not a shared one. Nothing in the design ties it there: each halo is driven by only two small classified values rather than a raw stream (Section~\ref{sec:study1-methods}), so it could equally run on a spectator wall, tabletop display, or shared dashboard \cite{morrisonsmith_ambiteam_2021, liu_animo_2019}. Whether a bystander reads that graded cue the way a teammate does, or instead as a public verdict, the very reading the STS commitments were built to head off, is untested and a natural next step.

\subsection{Design Implications and the Politics of Visibility}
Encoding relative to a personal baseline kept states legible without asserting a clinical reading, and withholding emotion labels still produced a legible display that gained user acceptance. Most consequential, control over visibility is a precondition rather than a refinement: participants accepted the display only when it remained optional, mutable, and subtle, and Study 2 confirmed why. Making a bodily signal visible changes the informational terms of play, and with them how blame, hesitation, and effort are read among teammates, the redistribution of accountability a politics of artifacts anticipates \cite{winner_artifacts_1980}. Participants in both studies asked for consent over who can see their status and the ability to withdraw it, recasting privacy from an afterthought into the interface's core logic; a deployed version would need to treat the right to be illegible as seriously as the capacity to be read.

\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limitations}

Study 1 alone had no comparison condition and simulated cardiovascular states rather than a live wearable feed; Study 2's three-condition design and live pipeline closed both gaps, though the result is mixed rather than a clean win for ambiguity, with the Icon Cue competitive on outcomes closest to the pressure events tested and behind the Numeric Cue on others (Section~\ref{sec:discussion}). Recognition accuracy remained moderate rather than near ceiling, with the middle states on each channel harder to separate than the endpoints, so a deployed system should budget for a comparable error rate. Study 2 relied on one device, the Apple Watch's photoplethysmography sensor and HealthKit-derived HRV, so behavior on other consumer wearables, whose accuracy and HRV estimation vary by device and vendor, remains untested; a deployed version also needs a fallback for players without a supported wearable, most simply a neutral, unanimated avatar.

Several limitations are specific to Study 2 or remain after it. Most consequentially, Study 2 reused Study 1's sample rather than recruiting a fresh one (Section~\ref{sec:study2-methods}), so the two studies are not independent evidence: every participant had already identified all 16 HR/HRV combinations from labels-hidden animation in Study 1, prior exposure that plausibly made the Icon Cue easier to read than it would be for a genuinely first-time viewer, while the Numeric Cue's plain bpm and millisecond values required no comparable training. Study 2's Icon-versus-Numeric comparisons on speed and awareness should therefore be read as a best case under practiced viewing conditions. We also did not conduct an a priori power analysis for the team-level behavioral contrasts, which drew on only 12 independent team units; the numerically, but not significantly, faster Icon-versus-Numeric-Cue comparison on support response time should be read with both this modest N and the practiced sample in mind, not as evidence of true equivalence. Its scripted pressure events, modeled on organic Overcooked-style bottlenecks \cite{carroll_utility_2019, baek_toward_2022}, were researcher-triggered on a fixed schedule rather than emergent, so support response time measures reaction to a controlled probe, not the full variety of overload a longer session would produce; each team played each condition once, for seven minutes, ruling out unequal practice through counterbalancing but leaving open whether either cue's benefit would hold across dozens of sessions. Coded communication relied on a single primary coder rather than an independently double-coded reliability sample. Both studies drew on a single convenience sample of 36 people from the author's university subject pool and personal network, voluntarily and without compensation, limiting generalization to a broader population; both relied on self report, without a physiological ground truth for the viewer; and the bespoke cooperative cooking task, built to avoid copyrighted commercial levels, leaves open whether the same pattern would hold in a shipped title. Finally, the privacy concerns Study 1 surfaced qualitatively, Study 2 confirmed quantitatively (Section~\ref{sec:s2-privacy}): a real deployment would still need to resolve consent, data ownership, and control over visibility, including the separate icon/numeric permissions several participants requested.

\section{Conclusion}
Distributed play removes the body from social interaction: teammates who would read one another's effort and strain at a glance in a shared room instead infer those states from text, voice, and avatar motion, and the resulting misreadings seed misplaced blame and broken coordination. This paper asked whether that missing bodily layer could be returned as a readable cue without collapsing it into a named emotion, and answered in two stages.

Study 1 showed that a label free display communicates well above chance, with readings ordered as designed and workload on the low side of the scale. Study 2 carried that icon system into live cooperative play and found it moved teams to support a struggling teammate measurably faster, matching an explicit numeric display on speed and awareness while costing less workload and invasiveness, though the numeric alternative came out ahead on coordination usefulness, trust, and mission score.

The contributions outlined in Section~\ref{sec:intro} stand as stated there. What Study 2 adds is a demonstration that a wearable driven, label free cue moves teammates to notice and support one another faster, at a real but bounded cost in precision and trust relative to explicit disclosure. Building the visibility controls players asked for, an immediate off switch and separate permissions for the icon and the exact numbers, into the interface's core is the design priority a deployed version would need to meet before this coordination benefit could be carried into practice. Every session here rendered that cue on one player's own screen; Section~\ref{sec:discussion} sets out testing it on a shared surface as the natural next step.

\section{Data and Materials Availability}
\label{sec:data-availability}
The anonymized trial-level datasets, stimulus animations, questionnaire item banks, communication-coding scheme, data-logging schema, Latin-square counterbalancing schedule, and analysis scripts for both studies will be deposited in a public repository upon publication, along with supplementary tables for results this paper reports only in prose or figures: full chance-level and trend-test statistics, participant and team demographics, behavioral outcomes and planned contrasts, questionnaire outcomes by item, system reliability logs, coded communication rates, and qualitative themes with example quotes.

\section{Generative AI Use Disclosure}
A generative AI tool (ChatGPT) was used for language editing and restructuring of text drafted by the author across both studies reported in this paper; it was not used to generate the research idea, conceptual framework, study designs, stimuli, data, statistical results, or citations, and it played no role in participant recruitment, data collection, or analysis for either study. The author reviewed and takes responsibility for all content produced with its assistance.
